patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 56-year-old female office administrator. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by volunteering: a weird painful red rash that comes and goes, mostly in the groin/butt area and sometimes around your mouth; unintentional weight loss for months; sore mouth with cracked lips; and a heavy/full feeling on the right side of your belly. If asked, add: increased thirst and urination, occasional blurry vision, fatigue, intermittent loose stools, increased appetite despite weight loss, and that the right side of your belly looks more swollen/enlarged. Do not mention unless specifically prompted: rash flares when you skip meals/fast, slow healing of cuts, smooth sore tongue with stinging foods, thinning hair. Be initially hesitant about genital involvement and lower sex drive; only share after reassurance/normalization. Express misconceptions: you thought it was yeast infection/eczema; creams help briefly; you wondered about stress, food allergy, or “random flares.” If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and become more willing to discuss embarrassing details. Bring up cost/time-off worries when tests or referrals are suggested.

Provide treatment counseling: expected benefits, side effects (GI upset, gallstones, glucose changes), hypoglycemia/hyperglycemia recognition and actions, injection technique basics, and when to seek urgent care.

confounders: Chronic hypoventilation from ROHHAD causing baseline hypercapnia and somnolence that can mimic/worsen encephalopathy
Autonomic dysregulation (labile temperature, heart rate, and blood pressure) complicating interpretation of fever and hemodynamics
Recent travel to a rural mosquito-endemic area with multiple possible arboviral exposures (e.g., West Nile, dengue) causing serologic cross-reactivity concerns
Hyponatremia consistent with SIADH in CNS infection, which can independently cause altered mental status and seizures

required_evidence: Compatible encephalitis syndrome (fever with altered mental status and/or seizures and/or focal neurologic deficits)
Epidemiologic risk (travel/residence in endemic rural Asia, mosquito exposure, unvaccinated status, seasonal exposure)
Laboratory evidence of infection: positive Japanese encephalitis virus (JEV) IgM in CSF (preferred) or serum, or rising neutralizing antibody titers on paired sera
Exclusion/low likelihood of key treatable alternatives (e.g., HSV encephalitis considered and empiric acyclovir started until ruled out)

Seizure precautions and management per protocol; treat status epilepticus if present
Evaluate for increased intracranial pressure and manage accordingly (head-of-bed elevation, avoid hypercapnia; neuroimaging/LP decisions per stability)
Infection control and public health notification per local requirements; counsel on mosquito avoidance and vaccination for contacts/travelers as appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 4-6 hours after admission and at least daily thereafter; review confirmatory serology/CSF results within 24-72 hours and de-escalate empiric antivirals once HSV is excluded; arrange neurology follow-up within 1-2 weeks after discharge and neurorehabilitation as needed

must_include: Japanese encephalitis is a mosquito-borne viral infection that can cause brain inflammation (encephalitis)
Diagnosis relies on symptoms plus exposure history and specific antibody testing (JEV IgM in CSF/serum); early results may be pending
Rett syndrome is a neurodevelopmental condition and does not explain an acute infectious encephalitis picture; it is considered only if there is a chronic developmental regression pattern
No proven virus-specific curative therapy for JEV; treatment is mainly supportive and focused on preventing complications
Empiric acyclovir is started initially to cover HSV encephalitis (a treatable emergency) until testing excludes it
Seizure control, fever control, and careful fluid/electrolyte management are key parts of care
Discuss potential complications (seizures, breathing problems, increased intracranial pressure, long-term neurologic deficits) and the need for close monitoring
Clarify medication safety: document amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy; current plan does not require penicillin-class antibiotics unless a bacterial infection is suspected
Explain expected course and uncertainty: recovery can be prolonged; rehabilitation may be needed

Return precautions after discharge: worsening confusion, new seizures, severe headache, persistent vomiting, weakness, or breathing difficulty

Review available results; recognize that JEV IgM may be pending early and interpret in context of exposure and clinical syndrome; ensure HSV testing status is known.
State working diagnosis of suspected Japanese encephalitis (or viral encephalitis with JEV high on differential) while maintaining treatable emergent differentials (HSV encephalitis) until excluded.
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm/document amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy and reaction details.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; assess for interactions with intended empiric and supportive meds (e.g., acyclovir, antiepileptics, antipyretics, antiemetics).

Initiate empiric acyclovir pending HSV exclusion and prescribe supportive care measures/meds as appropriate (e.g., antipyretics, antiseizure therapy if seizures, antiemetics, IV fluids with careful electrolyte management).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment (supportive + empiric HSV coverage), address concerns/complications/uncertainty and allergy safety, and provide mosquito prevention/vaccine counseling and return precautions.
Arrange close monitoring and follow-up planning (inpatient neuro/ID involvement as applicable; post-discharge neuro rehab and follow-up; ensure pending results review plan).
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, treatment initiation, education, and follow-up plan are completed.

patient.chief_complaint: I keep having these weird episodes where my vision goes funny and then I blank out for a bit.

volunteer: Episodes that start with flickering lights or a shimmering blind spot in both eyes, then I feel 'out of it' for a minute or two
After an episode I’m confused and wiped out for a while
Headaches that can be severe, sometimes with nausea and light sensitivity
if_asked: My partner says I sometimes stare, smack my lips, or fumble with my hands during the episodes
Sometimes one side of my body feels weak or heavy during or after a bad headache, and it can last hours
I occasionally lose words or can’t get sentences out right after an episode
Bright lights, lack of sleep, and stress seem to make the episodes more likely
I’ve had a few falls because I didn’t realize I was drifting to one side
I don’t always remember the whole event, like there are missing pieces
hidden: I bump into things on either side more than I should, like I don’t notice stuff until it’s close
Reading can be slow because lines seem to 'jump' or I lose my place
I get brief moments where sounds feel far away and time feels weird, even without a headache
I’ve had trouble with maps and judging distance since I was a kid, but I thought I was just bad at it
Sometimes I don’t notice a cut or bruise until later because I’m not sure when it happened
resistant: I’ve wet the bed once or twice after a nighttime episode (I’m really embarrassed about it)
I’m scared to tell people because I don’t want to lose my job or have my license taken away
I sometimes avoid showering alone because I’m afraid I’ll have an episode and fall
misleading: I’m pretty sure it’s just 'complicated migraines' because my family has migraines and I get the flashing lights
I’ve wondered if it’s panic attacks because my heart races and I feel unreal right before it happens
I thought maybe it was low blood sugar because it sometimes happens when I skip meals
noise: My neck and shoulders are tight from working at a computer all day
Occasional heartburn after spicy food
Seasonal allergies with a stuffy nose and itchy eyes

general_concerns: Because it’s a brain malformation, I assume it keeps getting worse over time and will eventually cause dementia.
I think the MRI means I have ‘holes’ in my brain that could suddenly collapse or bleed if I exercise or get stressed.
I’m worried the condition is contagious or caused by something I did (like past alcohol use or not taking enough vitamins) and that I can ‘reverse it’ with supplements or detoxes.
I assume seizures are inevitable and that any brief visual glitch or headache means I’m having silent seizures.
I believe pregnancy would automatically make the condition worse and that I shouldn’t have children because they will definitely inherit it.
economic_concerns: I’m afraid a documented brain condition will make me uninsurable or raise my premiums, so I’m hesitant to have it listed in my records.
I worry that if my employer finds out, I’ll lose my job or won’t be promoted because they’ll assume I’m unsafe or unreliable.
I think I need frequent repeat MRIs and expensive tests every year to ‘monitor progression,’ and I’m anxious about the cost.
I’m concerned that neurological medications, if prescribed, will be unaffordable long-term and I’ll have to choose between treatment and rent.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 27-year-old anxious but clear-speaking administrative assistant. Open with: “I keep having these weird episodes where my vision goes funny and then I blank out for a bit.” Early on, volunteer: flickering lights/shimmering blind spot in both eyes, then feeling “out of it” 1–2 minutes; afterward you’re confused and wiped out. Mention severe headaches with nausea and light sensitivity.

If asked about what others see, describe staring, lip smacking, fumbling with hands, missing pieces of memory, and a few falls/drifting to one side. If asked about neurologic symptoms, mention occasional one-sided weakness/heaviness lasting hours after bad headaches and trouble finding words right after episodes. Identify triggers: bright lights, lack of sleep, stress, sometimes skipping meals.

Do not volunteer: bedwetting after nighttime episodes, avoiding showering alone, or lifelong trouble with maps/distance/reading lines jumping—only admit if specifically asked, embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: “I think it’s complicated migraines,” “maybe panic attacks,” “maybe low blood sugar.” Share fears about job/license, insurance, and cost of MRIs/meds. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and become more willing to disclose.

medications: lamotrigine 100 mg PO twice daily
verapamil ER 120 mg PO nightly
magnesium oxide 400 mg PO daily
ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for headache (max 1200 mg/day OTC)
comorbidities: focal epilepsy secondary to cortical malformation (polymicrogyria)
confounders: hemiplegic migraine attacks with transient unilateral weakness and aphasia mimicking focal seizure or stroke
postictal (Todd) paresis after focal seizures causing transient focal deficits
intermittent visual aura/field symptoms from parieto-occipital involvement mimicking ophthalmologic disease
sleep deprivation and psychosocial stress as triggers for both seizures and migraine
mild hypomagnesemia that can lower seizure threshold and exacerbate migraine

correct_diagnosis.primary: Bilateral parasagittal parieto-occipital polymicrogyria (cortical malformation) with epilepsy risk
acceptable_alternatives: Malformation of cortical development (polymicrogyria) with neurodevelopmental sequelae
required_evidence: Brain MRI demonstrating bilateral parasagittal parieto-occipital polymicrogyria (irregular cortical surface with excessive small gyri and abnormal cortical thickening) in the described distribution
Clinical correlation for seizures (focal aware/impaired awareness, visual phenomena, secondary generalization) and/or developmental/neurologic deficits consistent with parieto-occipital involvement
EEG supportive of focal epileptiform activity (often posterior regions) if seizures are present/suspected
Exclude acquired mimics (e.g., perinatal stroke, infection) by history and imaging pattern when relevant

expected_lab_results.Sodium (Na+): Typically normal at baseline; monitor for hyponatremia if treated with oxcarbazepine/carbamazepine
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Typically normal; if treated with topiramate, may show mild non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (low CO2)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Typically normal; needed for renal dosing (e.g., levetiracetam)

medications: Levetiracetam 500 mg PO twice daily (titrate by 500 mg/day every 1-2 weeks as needed; typical target 1000-3000 mg/day)
Midazolam (intranasal) 5 mg once for seizure lasting >5 minutes; may repeat once after 10 minutes if still seizing (rescue medication)
non_pharmacological: Seizure safety counseling (no swimming/bathing alone, driving restrictions per local law, avoid heights/open flames, supervision during water activities)
Identify and reduce seizure triggers (sleep deprivation, alcohol/recreational drugs, missed doses, intercurrent illness)
Referral to epilepsy specialist/epilepsy center for characterization (EEG review, seizure classification) and consideration of additional therapies if drug-resistant
Neuropsychological/vision assessment and supportive therapies as indicated (OT/PT/speech; educational accommodations)
Genetic counseling/testing consideration for malformations of cortical development when appropriate
SUDEP counseling and risk-reduction strategies (optimize seizure control, adherence, nocturnal supervision options)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 4-6 weeks after initiation/titration (earlier within 1-2 weeks if adverse effects or ongoing seizures); urgent evaluation if status epilepticus, new focal deficits, or escalating seizure frequency

must_include: Polymicrogyria is a congenital malformation of the brain cortex (not a tumor or infection) affecting the parieto-occipital regions on both sides
Symptoms can include seizures and difficulties with vision/spatial processing, learning, or motor coordination depending on extent
Course varies; focus is on symptom control and supportive therapies
Goal is seizure prevention and safety; antiseizure medication is first-line when seizures are present or strongly suspected
How to take levetiracetam, expected timeline for titration, common side effects (fatigue, dizziness, mood/irritability) and when to call
Rescue medication indications and step-by-step use; when to call emergency services (seizure >5 minutes, repeated seizures without recovery, breathing issues, injury)
Discuss prognosis uncertainty and individualized monitoring
Review reproductive/teratogenic counseling if applicable (planning pregnancy requires medication review and folic acid; do not stop antiseizure meds abruptly)
Discuss mental health screening and support given potential mood effects of levetiracetam and chronic neurologic disease
Strict medication adherence and not stopping suddenly
Sleep hygiene and avoidance of alcohol/recreational substances that lower seizure threshold
Driving/work safety guidance and documentation per local regulations
SUDEP education and practical risk-reduction steps

Respiratory screening if symptoms/signs suggest hypoventilation (sleep history; consider spirometry) and swallow assessment if bulbar symptoms present

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 8-12 weeks after therapy initiation, then every 6-12 months (earlier if rapid change, falls, respiratory/swallow symptoms, or new functional decline)

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male forklift operator on light duty. You speak vaguely and are a bit evasive. Start by saying your legs and hips feel weaker and stairs/chairs/toilet are harder; mention tripping because your feet don’t lift well and you tire faster walking even without shortness of breath. Do NOT offer breathing, choking, nighttime symptoms, urine color, mood, incontinence, or sexual issues unless directly asked.
If asked about other weakness, admit shoulders are weaker overhead, a few falls, clumsy hands, cramps/tightness, occasional heart “flutter,” morning headaches/grogginess, and voice gets weak with long talking.
Only if specifically asked about breathing/swallowing/sleep: reveal you sleep propped up, feel you can’t take a deep breath lying flat, get winded talking, occasional choking/coughing with fast water, weak neck by day’s end, hunched posture/back ache, thigh/shoulder muscle loss, and dark urine after very hard days.
If asked gently about sensitive topics, reluctantly admit nighttime saliva choking, urgency accidents, anxiety/depression, and reduced sex.
Express misconceptions: think it’s back/hip pinched nerve; wonder about MS or thyroid; blame deconditioning.
If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly, but worry about costs, time off, and insurance/employment.

sertraline 50 mg once daily
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU once daily
confounders: deconditioning due to reduced mobility and neurodegenerative disease
vitamin D deficiency contributing to myalgias/weakness
mild cardiomyopathy/possible cardiac involvement (elevated NT-proBNP) that can accompany myofibrillar myopathy and complicate dyspnea/fatigue assessment
SSRI-associated fatigue (sertraline) potentially mimicking worsening weakness
history of seizures and cognitive impairment from neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis limiting reliability of symptom reporting

required_evidence: Progressive proximal muscle weakness with chronic course (often adult onset)
Myopathic pattern on EMG (if obtained) and/or MRI showing selective muscle involvement
Muscle biopsy consistent with MFM: myofibrillar disorganization with protein aggregates (e.g., desmin, myotilin, filamin C, BAG3) and Z-disk pathology
Genetic testing identifying a pathogenic variant in an MFM-associated gene (e.g., DES, MYOT, FLNC, BAG3, CRYAB, LDB3/ZASP, FHL1)
Exclusion of treatable mimics (inflammatory myopathy, endocrine causes) with appropriate labs/clinical features

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often <5x upper limit of normal); can be moderate in some genotypes
expected_lab_results.Aldolase: Normal to mildly elevated; parallels CK if elevated
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal to mildly elevated (may reflect muscle source if CK elevated)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal to mildly elevated (may reflect muscle source if CK elevated)
expected_lab_results.Troponin I (high-sensitivity): Usually normal; if elevated, evaluate for cardiac involvement (MFM can be associated with cardiomyopathy/arrhythmias depending on genotype)
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Usually normal; may be elevated if associated cardiomyopathy/heart failure present
expected_lab_results.TSH: Typically normal (used to exclude hypothyroid myopathy)

medications: Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease/alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neuromuscular specialist for confirmation (biopsy/genetics) and longitudinal management
Physical therapy: individualized low-to-moderate intensity strengthening and aerobic conditioning; avoid overexertion and eccentric-heavy regimens that worsen weakness
Occupational therapy for ADL optimization; assess need for assistive devices (cane, walker, orthoses) and fall-prevention plan

Baseline and periodic cardiopulmonary screening due to potential MFM-associated cardiomyopathy/arrhythmias and respiratory weakness: ECG, echocardiogram (and consider Holter), pulmonary function tests including FVC (upright/supine) and nocturnal oximetry if symptoms
Swallow evaluation if dysphagia symptoms; nutrition optimization and weight management
Genetic counseling and family screening discussion once genotype is known
Vaccinations and infection prevention; plan perioperative/anesthesia precautions if respiratory/cardiac involvement suspected
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular follow-up in 4-8 weeks to review diagnostic results and initiate multidisciplinary monitoring; sooner (1-2 weeks) if rapid progression, bulbar symptoms, dyspnea, syncope/palpitations, or markedly rising CK

must_include: Myofibrillar myopathy is a genetic/structural muscle disease causing gradual proximal weakness
Diagnosis typically requires muscle biopsy and/or genetic testing; blood tests alone are not definitive
Course is variable; some forms can involve heart rhythm/cardiac muscle and breathing muscles, so screening is important
Discuss key warning symptoms requiring urgent care: new/worsening shortness of breath, fainting, chest pain, palpitations, trouble swallowing, rapidly worsening weakness
No disease-specific cure; treatment focuses on function, safety, symptom control, and monitoring complications
Role of PT/OT and assistive devices to preserve mobility and prevent falls
Plan for cardiopulmonary evaluation and periodic reassessment
Avoid empiric long-term immunosuppression unless inflammatory myopathy is confirmed
Acknowledge uncertainty until biopsy/genetics results return and outline next diagnostic steps
Discuss inheritance possibilities and offer genetic counseling
Review medication safety, including confirming penicillin allergy history and documenting reaction (urticarial rash)
Energy conservation and pacing; avoid overexertion and prolonged immobilization
Home safety and fall prevention strategies
Maintain balanced nutrition and adequate protein intake; hydration and sleep optimization
Encourage appropriate, consistent exercise under PT guidance rather than complete rest

Order baseline neuromuscular labs to support evaluation and rule out mimics (e.g., CK, CMP/AST/ALT, TSH, HbA1c, CBC, vitamin D/B12 if relevant) and establish pre-treatment baseline; clarify that labs are not definitive for myofibrillar myopathy.
Review lab results to identify alternative diagnoses, degree of muscle injury, and any immediate abnormalities that change urgency or management.

Synthesize findings and communicate suspected myofibrillar myopathy vs differential; explicitly note that confirmation typically requires muscle biopsy and/or genetic testing and plan next diagnostic steps.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment approach (supportive, PT/OT, monitoring), address concerns (uncertainty, inheritance/genetic counseling, medication safety), and lifestyle guidance (pacing, fall prevention, nutrition, appropriate exercise).

Arrange cardiopulmonary screening plan (ECG/echo ± Holter; pulmonary function testing if symptoms) and neuromuscular follow-up for biopsy/genetic testing coordination; set reassessment interval and escalation plan.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting for urgent symptoms, and documenting the plan.

medications: Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily

confounders: Recent treated community-acquired pneumonia with incomplete response (can mimic COP)

Mildly elevated D-dimer raising concern for pulmonary embolism despite alternative diagnosis

acceptable_alternatives: Organizing pneumonia (pending exclusion of secondary causes)
required_evidence: Subacute (weeks) respiratory symptoms (cough and/or dyspnea) with systemic features (fatigue, low-grade fever) and lack of response to typical antibiotics
Inflammatory markers elevated (CRP/ESR) with procalcitonin typically low (argues against bacterial pneumonia)
CBC may show mild leukocytosis or be normal; no marked neutrophilia typical of bacterial infection

Clinical reasoning documents exclusion/low likelihood of common differentials (bacterial pneumonia, viral pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, heart failure, malignancy, drug-induced pneumonitis, connective-tissue disease–associated ILD) based on history/exam and available data
Plan includes confirmatory evaluation (e.g., chest imaging pattern consistent with organizing pneumonia and/or pulmonary consult/bronchoscopy/biopsy if atypical or not improving)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal (may be mildly low if chronic inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Low/normal (typically <0.25 ng/mL; supports non-bacterial inflammatory process)

Assess oxygenation and provide supplemental oxygen if hypoxemic
Arrange pulmonary follow-up and ensure chest imaging follow-up to document response
Vaccination review (influenza, pneumococcal) prior to or early in prolonged steroid course when feasible
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks for symptom response and inflammatory markers; earlier (48-72 hours) if worsening dyspnea, hypoxemia, or new fever; repeat imaging in ~4-8 weeks and during taper as clinically indicated

must_include: COP is an inflammatory lung condition where small airways/alveoli fill with organizing tissue, often after an infection but not an active bacterial pneumonia
Reason antibiotics may not help and why steroids are used
Need for follow-up to confirm improvement and exclude secondary causes if atypical or not responding
Prednisone plan (initial dose, expected improvement within days to 1-2 weeks, and slow taper over months)
Common steroid adverse effects (hyperglycemia, mood changes/insomnia, GI upset, fluid retention, infection risk) and when to seek care

Adherence to follow-up and monitoring (symptoms, vitals/oxygen if available)

Thinks lactic acidosis means he has "too much acid from soda or spicy food" and that avoiding acidic foods will fix it.
Believes vaccines might be unsafe for him because his immune system is weak, and that vaccines could "cause the infection."
Assumes getting out of breath during play means he is "out of shape" and should push harder to build stamina.
economic_concerns: Family worries that frequent lab tests and specialist visits are optional and can be skipped when he seems well to save money.
Concern that immune-related treatments (e.g., infusions/injections) are mainly for severe cases and not worth the cost if he is not currently sick.
Belief that using urgent care for fevers is cheaper and faster than having a consistent specialty care plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s parent bringing your 8-year-old son. Be cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “He keeps getting fevers out of nowhere every few weeks and seems more tired than usual.” Volunteer: fevers every few weeks lasting 2–3 days then resolving, low energy, pale look, short of breath when running, poor appetite during fever episodes.

If asked, add: several infections this year (ear infections/bad colds) that linger, mouth sores during fevers, belly pain, occasional vomiting with high fever, sometimes fast/deep breathing, once or twice unusually sleepy/hard to wake, prior labs showed “low blood counts,” and doctors mentioned “too acidic blood/lactic acidosis.”

Do not mention unless specifically probed: small size/poor weight gain, delayed walking/talking and learning/speech struggles, muscle weakness/climbing stairs, dark urine/yellow eyes after illnesses, fast heartbeat at rest, headaches/dizziness on standing.

Be reluctant about underwear soiling; only admit if directly asked. Express misconceptions: worry it’s Lyme, “flu coming back,” or juvenile arthritis due to joint aches.

If clinician shows empathy, thank them and say you’re worried and want a clear plan but costs matter; question if labs/specialists are necessary when he seems well and prefer urgent care for fevers.

Peripheral smear.value: Microcytosis, hypochromia, anisopoikilocytosis; occasional basophilic stippling

Bone marrow aspirate.value: Erythroid hyperplasia with ring sideroblasts (~35% of erythroid precursors)

Urinalysis.value: Specific gravity 1.020; ketones trace; leukocyte esterase negative; nitrite negative

medications: Immune globulin (IVIG) 500 mg/kg IV every 4 weeks
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 80/400 mg PO once daily (Pneumocystis prophylaxis)
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO once daily
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever
confounders: Recent decreased oral intake and mild dehydration during febrile episode (can worsen lactic acidosis)
Ongoing inflammatory flare/periodic fever elevating CRP/ESR and causing tachycardia
Recurrent respiratory infections in the setting of B-cell immunodeficiency mimicking sepsis despite negative early cultures
Iron overload from chronic anemia/transfusions can elevate ferritin and cause mild transaminitis

diagnosis.primary: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital sideroblastic anemia–B-cell immunodeficiency–periodic fever–developmental delay syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: Sideroblastic anemia due to SLC25A38-associated congenital sideroblastic anemia (if immunodeficiency/fever features not established)
required_evidence: Microcytic or dimorphic anemia with inappropriately low reticulocyte response
Elevated RDW consistent with mixed red cell populations (dimorphic picture common in sideroblastic anemia)
Clinical history consistent with syndromic features: recurrent periodic fevers, B-cell immunodeficiency/recurrent infections, developmental delay
Lactic acidosis suggesting mitochondrial/metabolic involvement
Exclusion of common acquired causes of microcytosis (iron deficiency, thalassemia trait) based on clinical context and available data

expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low or inappropriately normal relative to degree of anemia
expected_lab_results.MCV: Low (microcytic) or mixed microcytic/normocytic pattern if dimorphic
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly low; may fluctuate with febrile episodes
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Normal to mildly low; monitor for neutropenia during illness

medications: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO once daily
Folic acid 1 mg PO once daily
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/PR every 6 hours as needed for fever (max 60 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day, whichever lower)
0.9% sodium chloride IV bolus 10-20 mL/kg once, then reassess (for symptomatic lactic acidosis/dehydration or poor perfusion)
Packed red blood cell transfusion 10-15 mL/kg IV once (if symptomatic anemia, hemodynamic instability, or very low hemoglobin per institutional threshold)
non_pharmacological: Urgent evaluation and management of lactic acidosis: assess perfusion, hydration status, oxygenation; identify triggers such as infection, fasting, or medications
Avoid prolonged fasting; ensure adequate caloric intake during illness (sick-day plan) to reduce metabolic decompensation risk
Hematology referral for definitive workup/management (consider bone marrow evaluation for ring sideroblasts and genetic confirmation if not already established)
Immunology referral for B-cell immunodeficiency management (vaccination strategy, consideration of immunoglobulin replacement if indicated, infection prevention plan)
Fever plan: prompt assessment for infection with low threshold for cultures and empiric antibiotics when clinically indicated, tailored to allergy history
Developmental services referral (early intervention/therapy) and multidisciplinary care coordination
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1 week with hematology/immunology; sooner (same day/within 24 hours) if lactic acidosis persists, fever with toxicity, worsening anemia symptoms, or ANC low

must_include: The condition is a genetic/syndromic form of sideroblastic anemia causing the body to make red blood cells poorly, leading to anemia
The syndrome can also involve immune problems (B-cell immunodeficiency), periodic fevers, and developmental delays
Lactic acidosis can occur with metabolic/mitochondrial stress and needs prompt assessment and treatment
Trial of pyridoxine (vitamin B6) and folate support; response is monitored with blood counts/reticulocytes
When anemia is severe or symptomatic, transfusion may be needed
Lactic acidosis management focuses on hydration/perfusion, treating triggers (often infection), and close monitoring
Review amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy (urticarial rash) and avoid related beta-lactams if concern for IgE-mediated reaction; discuss alternative antibiotics if needed
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: lethargy, breathing difficulty, persistent vomiting, dehydration, worsening pallor, fainting, persistent high fever, or signs of sepsis
Avoid prolonged fasting; maintain regular meals and extra carbohydrates/fluids during illness per sick-day plan
Infection prevention strategies and when to seek early evaluation for fever due to immunodeficiency risk
Importance of scheduled follow-up and coordinated care with hematology/immunology and developmental services

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms and timeline (fever pattern, fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, vomiting/poor intake), sick-day triggers, hydration status, and red flags suggesting severe anemia, sepsis, or metabolic decompensation.

Clarify past history relevant to this syndrome: baseline hemoglobin/transfusion history, prior lactic acidosis episodes, infections/immunodeficiency history, developmental baseline, current meds/supplements (B6/folate), and family history.
Verify reported amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy details (urticarial rash, timing, severity) before any antibiotic prescribing; critical safety check.

Order evaluation for anemia severity and metabolic stress: CBC with differential, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear, CMP, lactate, venous/arterial blood gas, iron studies, CRP/ESR; add blood culture/urinalysis if febrile.
Interpret results to determine severity (Hb level, retic response), presence of lactic acidosis, and evidence of infection/inflammation.
Synthesize findings as congenital sideroblastic anemia syndrome with/without acute complication (symptomatic anemia, infection-triggered lactic acidosis, dehydration).
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and syndrome features; explain lactic acidosis risk; provide red-flag symptoms and sick-day guidance; discuss coordinated care and follow-up.
If prescribing new medications, screen for clinically meaningful drug interactions (non-critical but expected when available).

Initiate/continue supportive therapy (pyridoxine trial, folate) and treat triggers (e.g., non-beta-lactam antibiotic if infection suspected and allergy suggests IgE risk); ensure transfusion escalation plan if severe/symptomatic anemia.
Arrange close follow-up with hematology/immunology and developmental services; plan monitoring labs (CBC/retic) and fever evaluation pathway.
Close after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and documenting plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 52-year-old warehouse shift supervisor. Be vague and a bit evasive; minimize details unless directly asked. Start by saying: “I can’t stop having watery diarrhea, like tea-colored water, and I feel like I’m drying out.” Volunteer: months of frequent large-volume watery diarrhea, intense thirst, crampy belly discomfort, weakness/lightheadedness on standing, and unintentional weight loss (“everything runs right through me”).

Only if asked, add: diarrhea even when not eating, wakes you at night, no blood, rare mucus, occasional nausea, muscle cramps/twitching, racing heart, brief flushing of face/upper chest, dark scant urine, OTC anti-diarrheals and dairy/gluten cuts didn’t help, occasional RUQ ache after meals.

Resist/avoid: accidents/leakage, intimacy avoidance, past “colon cleanse” teas/laxatives—admit only if asked gently.

Express misconceptions: worry about food allergy; mention cousin’s “swelling attacks” and wonder if inherited; suspect low calcium/parathyroid; tried low-fat diet for a “family fat problem.”

If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but pivot to cost/work concerns; push for cheap symptomatic treatment and delaying imaging.

Stool sodium.normal_range: variable; often elevated in secretory diarrhea

medications: metformin 500 mg PO twice daily
lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
loperamide 4 mg PO once then 2 mg after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day) PRN
potassium chloride 20 mEq PO twice daily
confounders: Chronic proton pump inhibitor use can cause diarrhea and hypomagnesemia, mimicking/compounding secretory diarrhea and electrolyte abnormalities
Metformin-associated diarrhea complicates attribution of chronic watery diarrhea
Recent self-treatment with high-dose loperamide may partially reduce stool frequency and delay evaluation while increasing risk of arrhythmia in the setting of severe hypokalemia
Dehydration with prerenal azotemia can obscure baseline renal function and complicate interpretation of electrolyte disturbances

acceptable_alternatives: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor secreting VIP (VIP-secreting NET)
WDHA syndrome (Verner-Morrison syndrome) due to VIPoma
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting serum VIP (typically >200 pg/mL) in the setting of secretory watery diarrhea
Electrolyte pattern consistent with secretory diarrhea: hypokalemia and non–anion gap metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate) with hyperchloremia
Clinical dehydration/prerenal azotemia pattern (elevated BUN/creatinine ratio) if volume depleted
Anion gap not elevated (helps distinguish from high–anion gap acidoses)

Exclusion of key mimics suggested by provided differentials (no episodic angioedema/airway swelling for hereditary angioedema; no hypercalcemia pattern for primary parathyroid hyperplasia; no infantile neurodegenerative course for Sandhoff disease; no pancreatitis/chylomicronemia pattern for familial LPL deficiency)

expected_lab_results.Serum VIP: Elevated, typically >200 pg/mL (often markedly elevated in active disease); should be measured fasting
expected_lab_results.Sodium (Na+): Often low-normal to mildly low due to GI losses and free water intake; may be normal if dehydration predominates
expected_lab_results.Chloride (Cl-): High relative to sodium (hyperchloremia) consistent with non–anion gap metabolic acidosis from bicarbonate loss
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2/HCO3-): Low (metabolic acidosis due to bicarbonate loss in stool)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: May be elevated with dehydration/prerenal azotemia; improves with volume repletion

medications: 0.9% sodium chloride IV bolus 1-2 L, then maintenance infusion titrated to urine output and hemodynamics
Potassium chloride IV 10-20 mEq/h (with continuous ECG monitoring if >10 mEq/h) or PO replacement as tolerated; recheck K+ frequently
Octreotide 100-200 mcg subcutaneous every 8 hours (or IV infusion 25-50 mcg/hour if severe) to reduce secretory diarrhea; titrate to stool output
Sodium bicarbonate IV only if severe acidemia or symptomatic (e.g., HCO3- very low) while definitive control of diarrhea is initiated; otherwise correct with volume and diarrhea control
non_pharmacological: Strict intake/output monitoring; quantify stool volume to assess response
Frequent electrolyte and renal function monitoring (Na+, K+, Cl-, HCO3-, BUN/Cr), initially every 4-6 hours if unstable, then daily when improving
Hold/avoid medications that worsen diarrhea or potassium loss (e.g., stimulant laxatives, unnecessary diuretics)

Arrange definitive tumor localization and management after stabilization (cross-sectional imaging and somatostatin receptor imaging; surgical evaluation for resection if localized; oncology referral for systemic therapy if metastatic)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours for symptom and electrolyte response; outpatient endocrine/oncology and surgical follow-up within 1-2 weeks after stabilization (earlier if persistent high-volume diarrhea or refractory electrolyte derangements)

must_include: VIPoma is a rare hormone-secreting neuroendocrine tumor that produces vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP)
VIP causes profuse watery secretory diarrhea leading to dehydration, low potassium, and low bicarbonate (metabolic acidosis)
The diagnosis is supported by high fasting serum VIP together with the electrolyte/acid-base pattern
Immediate priorities are IV fluids and electrolyte replacement to prevent arrhythmias and kidney injury
Octreotide reduces VIP-driven diarrhea and helps correct potassium and bicarbonate losses
Definitive management requires locating and treating the tumor (often surgery if localized; other therapies if spread)
Review allergy history (penicillin and shellfish) and reassure that planned medications are not penicillin-based; advise to report any hives, swelling, or breathing difficulty
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent care: worsening weakness, palpitations, fainting, confusion, inability to keep up with fluids, reduced urination
Set expectations that diarrhea control may improve quickly with octreotide but tumor workup/treatment may take longer
Hydration plan and oral rehydration strategies while diarrhea is active
Avoid over-the-counter laxatives and review any supplements that could worsen diarrhea
Importance of follow-up labs and adherence to octreotide regimen if prescribed

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for secretory diarrhea syndrome (volume, duration, nocturnal symptoms, fasting effect), dehydration, red flags, weight loss, medication/supplement use, and differential considerations; establish baseline for severity and safety.

Order initial evaluation for watery diarrhea with suspected VIPoma: BMP/CMP (K, HCO3, creatinine), Mg, phosphate, CBC, venous/arterial blood gas if severe, stool electrolytes/osmotic gap if available, fasting serum VIP, and ECG if hypokalemia suspected.

Review labs to confirm the characteristic pattern (hypokalemia, low bicarbonate/metabolic acidosis, dehydration/AKI risk) and elevated fasting VIP supporting diagnosis; use results to guide urgent replacement.
Make/justify diagnosis of VIPoma (WDHA syndrome) based on clinical secretory diarrhea plus elevated fasting VIP and electrolyte/acid-base abnormalities; document key differentials considered.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing octreotide and electrolyte replacement; screen for interactions (e.g., glucose-lowering meds, beta-blockers, QT-prolonging agents if severe electrolyte derangements).

Initiate immediate management: IV fluids, potassium (and magnesium as needed), bicarbonate if indicated, and octreotide to reduce VIP-mediated diarrhea; consider antiemetics as needed; ensure dosing/route appropriate to severity.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment priorities, address allergy/urgent warning signs/expectations, and provide lifestyle/hydration and medication adherence guidance.

Arrange close follow-up for repeat electrolytes/renal function, response to octreotide, and definitive tumor localization/management (cross-sectional imaging, somatostatin receptor imaging, oncology/surgery referral).
Close encounter after ensuring safety net, understanding, and follow-up plan.

patient.chief_complaint: I keep getting these brutal one-sided headaches around my eye that hit out of nowhere.

volunteer: Severe stabbing pain behind my left eye that ramps up fast and feels unbearable
The attacks come in clusters for a few weeks, then I’m mostly fine for months
My left eye waters and my nose gets stuffy on the same side when it happens
I get really restless during the attacks—I can’t lie still
My dad had the same kind of headaches when he was my age
if_asked: Each attack lasts about 45 minutes to 1.5 hours
It often hits at nearly the same time at night and wakes me up from sleep
The pain is always on the same side and in the same spot
Bright light and sounds bother me a little during attacks, but the main issue is the eye/temple pain
I don’t usually feel nauseated or throw up with these
Alcohol can trigger an attack when I’m in one of these ‘bad stretches’
During the pain my eyelid droops a bit and the eye looks red on that side
Between these headache stretches, I can drink alcohol without problems
Over-the-counter pain pills barely touch it
I’ve had separate ‘electric shock’ face pains before (my trigeminal neuralgia), but this is different and lasts much longer
hidden: I’ve noticed my pupil looks smaller on the painful side during attacks (I only caught it in the mirror once)
I sometimes sweat more on the painful side of my forehead during an attack
Right before an attack in this cycle, I get a vague uneasy feeling and start pacing without knowing why
During this headache cycle, I’ve been avoiding naps because I’m scared it’ll trigger an attack
In the weeks leading up to this cluster, my sleep schedule got irregular and the headaches started soon after
resistant: I’ve been using more alcohol than I should to ‘knock myself out’ because I’m terrified of the nighttime attacks
I’ve taken a few of a friend’s leftover strong pain pills once or twice because I felt desperate
I’m worried I’m going to lose my job because I’m exhausted and missing mornings after these nights
misleading: I thought it might be a sinus infection because my nose runs and feels blocked on that side during the headaches
I wondered if it’s a tooth problem because the pain can spread into my upper jaw and cheek
I’ve been calling it a ‘migraine’ because it’s a really bad headache and light can feel annoying
noise: My neck and shoulders have been tight from stress lately
I’ve had some heartburn after spicy foods this month
My seasonal allergies have been acting up with sneezing on and off

general_concerns: Because his father had the same headaches, he believes this is an inherited condition that cannot be treated and that he just has to 'ride it out.'
He thinks the severe one-sided pain means something is 'bleeding in the brain' and worries that each attack is causing permanent damage.
He assumes cluster headache is basically a sinus infection or allergy problem because it comes with tearing and nasal congestion on one side.
He believes taking frequent over-the-counter painkillers early in an attack should stop it, and is confused why they don’t work.
He thinks stress is the main cause and that if he were calmer the attacks would stop, leading to guilt and self-blame.
economic_concerns: He is worried that treatments like oxygen therapy or injectable medications will be too expensive or not covered by insurance.
He avoids urgent care or emergency visits due to copays and fear of surprise bills, even during severe attacks.
He is concerned that taking time off work for appointments or attacks will jeopardize his job performance and income.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking IT support specialist. Start with: “I keep getting brutal one-sided headaches around my left eye that hit out of nowhere.” Volunteer early: severe stabbing left eye/temple pain that ramps up fast, unbearable; comes in clusters for a few weeks then months mostly fine; left eye waters and same-side nose gets stuffy; you’re restless and can’t lie still; your dad had similar headaches. If asked, give details: attacks last 45–90 minutes, often same time at night waking you; always same side/spot; little light/sound sensitivity; usually no nausea/vomiting; alcohol triggers during a cluster but not between; eyelid droops and eye gets red; OTC meds don’t help; different from your past trigeminal neuralgia “electric shocks.” Withhold unless specifically asked: smaller pupil, one-sided sweating, vague uneasy pre-attack feeling, avoiding naps, irregular sleep before this cycle. Resist admitting at first: increased alcohol to sleep, tried a friend’s strong pain pills, job risk from exhaustion—share only if asked about coping/substances or safety. Express misconceptions: you suspect sinus/tooth issue and call it “migraine.” If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fear of night attacks and money/insurance worries about oxygen/injections and ER bills.

medications: Carbamazepine 200 mg PO twice daily
Verapamil ER 240 mg PO nightly
Sumatriptan 6 mg SC as needed at headache onset (max 2 doses/24 h)
Oxygen 100% via non-rebreather 12-15 L/min for 15-20 min as needed for attacks
confounders: Trigeminal neuralgia causing overlapping unilateral facial pain symptoms
Seasonal alcohol use triggering attacks and mimicking sinus-related headache patterns
Tobacco use (current smoker) associated with cluster headache and can bias attribution of symptoms
Intermittent nasal congestion/rhinorrhea during attacks may be misattributed to allergic rhinitis or sinusitis
Carbamazepine–verapamil interaction (verapamil can increase carbamazepine levels) complicating symptom assessment due to dizziness/ataxia side effects

acceptable_alternatives: Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia consistent with cluster headache
required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attack frequency from 1 every other day up to 8 per day, occurring in clusters over weeks-months with remission (episodic pattern) OR without remission >3 months (chronic pattern)
Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms during attacks (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis)
Marked restlessness or agitation during attacks
No better explanation by secondary headache (no red flags; normal neuro exam; consider imaging if first presentation/atypical)

expected_lab_results.CMP (Creatinine): Within reference range (baseline renal function adequate for verapamil/lithium considerations)
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal/low (not suggestive of giant cell arteritis)

medications: Oxygen 100% via non-rebreather mask 12-15 L/min inhaled for 15-20 minutes at attack onset

Prednisone 60 mg oral daily for 5 days, then taper by 10 mg every 2-3 days (bridge therapy while verapamil is titrated)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster period (common trigger)
Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Home oxygen setup education (tank safety, non-rebreather use, timing at onset)
Headache diary to track timing, triggers, response to therapies
Consider referral to neurology/headache specialist; consider MRI brain with pituitary views if first episode/atypical features
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 1-2 weeks for response and verapamil titration (with ECG), sooner if worsening, new neurologic symptoms, chest pain, or inadequate abortive control

must_include: Definition of cluster headache and typical pattern (attacks in clusters with remission vs chronic)
Key features: unilateral severe pain with autonomic symptoms and restlessness
Familial predisposition is possible; not caused by penicillin allergy
Red flags that would prompt urgent reassessment/imaging (new neuro deficits, abrupt worst headache, fever/neck stiffness, vision loss, age >50 with new headache)
Abortive plan: high-flow oxygen at onset and how to use it; expected rapid relief
Triptan use: correct route (SC preferred), timing, maximum daily dose, and when not to use (e.g., ischemic heart disease, uncontrolled HTN, stroke history)
Preventive plan: verapamil as first-line prevention; need for dose titration and ECG monitoring for heart block/bradycardia
Bridge therapy: short steroid taper to reduce attacks while prevention is titrated; key adverse effects (insomnia, mood changes, hyperglycemia, GI upset)
Assess cardiovascular history and current chest pain/shortness of breath before triptan
Discuss medication side effects and what to do if they occur (e.g., palpitations, syncope with verapamil; chest tightness with triptan)
Confirm understanding of penicillin allergy and reassure that proposed meds are unrelated; instruct to report any new allergic symptoms
Set expectations: prevention reduces frequency over days-weeks; abortive treatments treat individual attacks
Avoid alcohol during active cluster periods
Sleep regularity and trigger avoidance
Use headache diary and bring to follow-up
When to seek urgent care: neurologic deficits, severe persistent headache unlike usual, chest pain after triptan, oxygen not helping or escalating frequency

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype and confirm cluster headache features, timing, frequency, triggers, associated autonomic symptoms/restlessness, and screen for secondary headache red flags.

Assess relevant medical history for treatment safety: cardiovascular/cerebrovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, pregnancy status if applicable, smoking, current chest pain/SOB, and current medication list (including OTC/herbals).
State working diagnosis of episodic vs chronic cluster headache based on the pattern and rule-out considerations; document differential and red-flag status.
Perform allergy check prior to prescribing; clarify penicillin allergy details and ensure no relevant allergies to proposed therapies.
Check for drug-drug interactions prior to prescribing (e.g., verapamil with beta-blockers, digoxin; triptans with ergotamines/MAOIs; other QT/AV-node agents).

Complete contraindication screening prior to prescribing: triptan contraindications (ischemic heart disease, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled HTN, hemiplegic/basilar migraine concern), verapamil contraindications (significant bradycardia, AV block, severe hypotension, HFrEF depending on context), steroid cautions (diabetes, infection, peptic ulcer).

Initiate evidence-based abortive and preventive therapy: high-flow oxygen; SC sumatriptan (or intranasal alternative if needed) with clear dosing limits; verapamil prevention with titration plan and ECG monitoring; short steroid taper as bridge when appropriate.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (pattern, features, familial predisposition), explain treatment (oxygen, triptan use/limits/contraindications, verapamil titration + ECG, steroid bridge + adverse effects), address concerns (CV screening, side effects, penicillin allergy reassurance), and lifestyle/urgent return precautions.
Arrange follow-up to assess response, review headache diary, titrate verapamil, and ensure ECG monitoring; provide earlier reassessment if worsening/red flags.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.
